title: What Does a 'Test-Optional' College Policy Actually Mean for You?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You hear the news: colleges are going test-optional. You breathe a sigh of relief. You hate standardized tests. You never have to take the SAT or ACT again. Not so fast. Test-optional does not mean test-blind. Here is what the policy actually means for your application.

Test-Optional vs. Test-Blind

Test-optional means you can choose whether to submit your scores. The college will review your application either way. If you submit strong scores, they help. If you submit weak scores, they hurt. If you submit nothing, the college focuses on other parts of your application.

Test-blind means the college will not consider scores even if you submit them. Very few schools are test-blind. The University of California system is test-blind. Caltech is test-blind. Most test-optional schools are not test-blind.

If you have strong scores, submit them. They can only help. If you have weak scores, don't submit them. That's the advantage of test-optional.

Who Should Submit Scores

Submit your scores if:
• Your scores are above the median for that college
• Your scores are strong relative to your GPA (e.g., high scores, lower GPA)
• You're applying for competitive scholarships that require scores
• You're applying to engineering or business programs that still value scores
• Your high school is not well-known to colleges (scores provide context)

A strong score can distinguish you from other applicants. It's extra evidence of your academic readiness. Even at test-optional schools, many students still submit scores.

Who Should Not Submit Scores

Do not submit your scores if:
• Your scores are below the 25th percentile for that college
• You have a strong GPA and rigorous course load (that will carry you)
• You're a poor test-taker and your practice scores are consistently low
• The time and stress of test prep would harm your grades or mental health

If you don't submit scores, your other materials need to be strong. Your essays, activities, and recommendations matter more. Use the time you would have spent on test prep to improve those areas.

What the Data Says

At test-optional schools, students who submit scores are admitted at higher rates than those who don't. But this is correlation, not causation. Students with strong scores also tend to have strong GPAs and activities. They would have been admitted anyway.

Some studies show that test-optional policies increase diversity. Students from low-income backgrounds are more likely to opt out. Their applications are evaluated on other merits.

If you're from a well-resourced school with strong grades and activities, not submitting scores is unlikely to hurt you. If you're from a school with less rigor or fewer resources, scores can provide valuable context.

The Scholarship Angle

Many scholarships require test scores. National Merit, state-funded scholarships, and university merit aid often use scores as criteria. If you need scholarships, you probably need to take the test.

Even if the college is test-optional, the scholarship committee might not be. Check the requirements for each scholarship you're targeting.

How to Decide

Take a practice test under timed conditions. See where you score. Compare your score to the middle 50% range for your target schools (published on their websites).

If you're above the median, take the real test and submit your scores. If you're below, decide whether you have time to improve. If you're already a senior, focus on other parts of your application.

Test-optional gives you choice. Use it wisely. Don't submit scores that hurt you. Don't hide scores that help you. And remember: test scores are just one part of your application. They are not the most important part.
